blurb: Strawflower opens stiff, papery 'everlasting' bracts in hot golds, oranges, reds, and pinks that feel crisp to the touch and dry perfectly for winter arrangements.
spread: Upright clump; does not run, may self-sow lightly
seasons: Papery daisy-like flowerheads from early summer to first frost; gray-green foliage spring–summer; frost-tender, killed by the first hard freeze
wildlife: Open flowers attract butterflies and bees; seedheads draw small birds
toxic: No known toxicity and no known edible use — grown ornamentally and for dried flowers
habitat: Open grassland and open woodland across much of Australia

care.hardiness: Hardy only in USDA zones 8–10, where it can persist as a short-lived tender perennial; on the Front Range it is grown as a warm-season annual and is killed by the first hard frost.[1]
care.planting: A frost-tender annual: plant out or sow only after Boulder's average last frost (about May 10–15) and once the soil has warmed.[2] Start seed indoors 4–6 weeks earlier and harden off for the earliest bloom, or direct-sow once it's warm. It is heat- and drought-tolerant and well suited to hot, sunny Front Range beds. In containers use a sunny, sharply drained pot; potted plants dry faster in our heat and wind, so water more often. No overwintering outdoors — it dies at frost.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for the most flowers and sturdiest stems; it tolerates partial shade (2–6 hours) but blooms less.[1]
care.soil: Average, well-drained loam; easily grown in average to dry soils and tolerant of pH from acidic to alkaline, so Boulder's alkaline clay is fine with improved drainage. It dislikes wet feet.[1]
care.water: Low — drought-tolerant once established. Keep seedlings moist, then water deeply but infrequently; it is happiest on the dry side and resents soggy soil.[1]
care.spacing: Space plants about 9–18 in. apart depending on the cultivar (dwarf types ~12 in., tall cut-flower types wider); each forms an upright clump 1–3 ft tall.[1]
care.sow: Start seed indoors 4–6 weeks before the last frost or direct-sow after frost (mid-to-late May in Boulder). The seed needs light to germinate, so press it onto the surface and leave it barely covered.[1][2]
care.depth: Surface-sow — barely cover the seed (no more than a sprinkle of fine soil) since light aids germination; keep evenly moist and warm until it sprouts.[1]
care.bloom: From early summer until frost — a long season of papery bracted flowerheads in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white.[1]
care.feeding: Light feeding is plenty; this is a low-maintenance plant that performs well on lean, dry soil and does not need heavy fertilizing.[1]
care.maintenance: Low-maintenance. Deadhead to keep new flowers coming; taller selections may need staking in windy, exposed spots. Pull and compost after frost.[1]
care.selfsow: May self-sow lightly in open, warm ground, but Front Range frost limits volunteers and seedlings are easy to thin; it forms a clump and does not run.[1]
care.harvest: For 'everlasting' dried flowers, cut stems just as the bracts begin to open (they keep opening as they dry), strip the leaves, bundle loosely, and hang upside down in a dark, airy place — the bracts hold their shape and color for months.[1]
care.propagation: Grown from seed (started indoors or direct-sown); stems can also be rooted from cuttings. Save seed from spent heads in fall and sow the next spring after frost.[1]

edible.summary: Strawflower is an ornamental grown for its papery 'everlasting' flowers — there is no documented edible use, and it is not eaten.[1]
edible.caution: Xerochrysum bracteatum is cultivated purely for its dried and cut flowers and has no known culinary use; it is not reported as a food plant, so do not eat any part of it. It is not, however, listed as notably poisonous to people or pets — it simply has no edible use. As with any non-food plant, keep flowers and foliage out of reach of small children and pets, and don't substitute it for an edible look-alike daisy.[1]
